title: Glasgow Prestwick Airport to target India as it seeks to expand cargo operations

sections.heading: What happened
sections.content: Glasgow Prestwick Airport said it is targeting India for cargo growth because demand is rising for Scottish salmon and whisky. Speaking at the Air Cargo Southeast Asia Expo in Singapore, business development director Nico Le Roux said the plan builds on progress toward the UK-India Free Trade Agreement.

sections.heading: Key details
sections.content: Prestwick said the agreement will remove tariffs on Scottish salmon and cut duties on Scotch whisky from 150 per cent to 75 per cent, with further cuts expected. Le Roux said the Indian subcontinent is a key target for 2026 and that the airport is actively seeking full freighter partners to mirror its collaborations with Chinese carriers.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.content: He said Prestwick could also serve as a European gateway for Indian pharmaceutical, medical equipment and garment industries. Industry figures cited by the airport showed India imported the equivalent of 192 million bottles of Scotch whisky in 2024, worth about GBP 248 million, while Salmon Scotland said tariffs on Scottish salmon could fall from around 30 per cent to zero. The same trade body said India's seafood market consumed nearly 12 million metric tonnes in 2021.

sourceReferences.citationText: serper-scottish-aviation – Glasgow Prestwick Airport to target India as it seeks to expand cargo operations
